steps.text: Determine if the licensee’s procedures for radiation protection at the facility meet regulatory requirements, and licensee commitments.
Determine if Form NRC-3, "Notice to Employees," is posted as required.
Determine if restricted areas are posted and controlled as required.
Determine if appropriate protective clothing is used by facility personnel as required.
Determine if the licensee’s personnel dosimetry program is effective, maintained and meets regulatory requirements and licensee commitments.
Determine if licensee personnel, equipment and materials exiting a controlled area are monitored as required.
Determine if personnel working with or around radioactive materials have been instructed in radiation protection as required.
Determine if required radiation surveys, sampling and monitoring were performed in accordance with regulatory requirements and licensee procedures.
Determine if the licensee has implemented a calibration program for the survey, sampling and monitoring instruments in use as required.
Determine if exposure limits in 10 CFR Part 20 were satisfied.
Determine if changes with the potential to affect radiation protection activities were consistent with 10 CFR 50.59, the license and Technical Specification (TS) requirements.
Determine if the licensee addressed radiologically significant events in accordance with regulatory and the licensee’s procedural requirements.
Determine if an ALARA program has been implemented as required.
Determine if the licensee has developed, documented and implemented a radiation protection program in accordance with regulatory requirements (see 10 CFR 20.1101).
Determine if the respiratory protection program meets regulatory requirements (see 10 CFR 20.1701-4) and licensee commitments.
Determine if planned special exposures were made in accordance with regulatory requirements (see 10 CFR 20.1206) and licensee procedures.
Determine if the dose to the embryo/fetus for declared pregnant women met regulatory requirements (see 10 CFR 20.1208).
Inform appropriate management to determine the course of action if there are concerns relative to other license activities or conditions.
Verify inspection requirements using direct observation if practicable, or otherwise use some combination of records review and independent measurement.
Consult with responsible management and the NRR project manager.
Review the changes to the facility's radiation protection procedures.
Verify Form NRC-3 is conspicuously posted.
Ask the licensee where Form NRC-3 is posted.
Directly observe the postings in one or two areas.

Observe the controls for two radiologically controlled areas, including reactor-related access control areas and areas where radioactive materials are used or stored (such as laboratories, chemical fume hoods, radwaste areas and receptacles).

Evaluate the licensee’s use of controls for radiation areas unique to Research and Test reactors, such as pneumatic irradiation sample transfer systems for 'rabbits,' beam ports, and other potential radiation work areas for experimental programs.
Evaluate the licensee’s use of shielding and controls used to remove or modify shielding.
Observe the protective clothing worn by two facility experimenters or other respective personnel during their work activities.
Conduct discussions with two individuals to determine their use of protective clothing.
Verify if personnel are instructed in the proper use and function of the protective clothing in accordance with 10 CFR 19.12.
Verify if the licensee has provisions for the correct disposition of contaminated protective clothing.
Observe the personnel dosimetry worn by four facility workers, including one operator and one experimenter.
Verify whether the dosimetry devices issued to facility personnel are appropriate for the type and energy of the emitted radiation and the radiation fields anticipated.
Verify whether facility personnel required to use dosimeters were instructed in the proper use and function of the dosimeters.

Accompany a person qualified in the licensee's radiation control techniques on normal rounds, or request to be shown the manner in which such tasks are normally accomplished, to observe two radiological surveys, sampling, or monitoring activities.
Determine by direct observation and discussion whether personnel conducting these activities are qualified by training and experience.
Verify that operability checks, such as source and battery checks, are performed on all instrumentation before use.

Determine if technicians take smears or instrument readings, as appropriate, in areas readily accessible to facility personnel, giving particular attention to beam ports, experimental stations, and ion exchangers.
Examine the licensee's training records to confirm that technicians are sufficiently trained.
Determine whether surveys were performed as required by the licensee's procedures and if exposure rates are consistent with the ALARA program.
Evaluate personnel occupancy of uncontrolled areas and the potential dose to occupants.
Check for excessive levels of contamination within restricted areas and at the exits from the restricted area.
Establish a barrier if contamination levels exceed double the background (unless the contamination is removable and the area is immediately decontaminated).
Perform independent radiological surveys as needed to verify licensee assumptions or measurements.
Compare the inspector's measurements with those completed by the licensee in the same areas to verify consistency.
Review surveys conducted by the licensee during both shutdown and operations following changes in shielding.
Verify reasonable agreement with the licensee’s records.
Determine how the licensee assures that levels are within the regulatory requirements of 10 CFR Part 20, Subparts C and D.
Examine surveys of solid waste material to determine whether the licensee complies with their procedures and requirements.
Verify the calibration of radiation monitoring instrument(s) used in the licensee’s radiation surveys.
Verify if all survey, sampling, and monitoring instruments have current calibrations appropriate for the types and energies of radiation to be detected.
Examine the technical adequacy of calibration procedures at facilities that perform their own calibrations.
Review personnel dosimetry records (using a sample of 50 percent of the facility's records).
Review the issue further to determine that the radiation exposure is consistent with the licensee’s ALARA program.

Determine if the licensee reported the over exposures in accordance with 10 CFR Parts 20.2202 and 20.2203, how the licensee addressed the problem, and inform appropriate management and the responsible NRR project manager.
Review major changes in organization, personnel, facilities, equipment, programs, and procedures that could affect radiation protection activities since the last inspection.
Review any organizational change involving the radiation protection program.
Give particular attention to the reporting chain above the facility director.
Be sensitive to changes that reduce the ability of the radiation protection.
Assess procedural compliance, technical performance, implementation, and the effectiveness of the facility radiation protection program.
Verify if facility supervisors periodically perform observations of the effectiveness of the staff in radiological work practices, work monitoring, procedural compliance, and survey adequacy.
Verify that the radiation protection program is periodically reviewed as required by 10 CFR 20.1101(c).
Verify that audits are performed by the radiation safety group in accordance with their program requirements.
Verify that the safety committee audits and evaluates the radiation protection program to assure compliance with 10 CFR Part 20, the TSs, and other program requirements.
Verify that respiratory protection equipment (and other controls) are used to limit intakes of radioactive material consistent with ALARA.
Verify compliance with requirements for planned special exposures, if applicable.
Verify compliance with requirements for the dose to the embryo/fetus and exposures to declared pregnant women.

preconditions.text: The inspection is for radiological controls at Class I Research and Test reactors, and not for NRC or Agreement State material licenses.
There are concerns relative to other license activities or conditions.
Regulatory action is about to be taken
Postings of Form NRC-3 are not readily apparent
If observation of protective clothing worn by personnel is possible
If observation of protective clothing worn by personnel is not practical
Concerns arise regarding whether technicians are sufficiently trained

Highly radioactive experiments or unshielded objects are adjacent to a wall near uncontrolled areas (such as laboratories, classrooms, or lounges) with radiation levels above 10 CFR Part 20, Subpart D.
Surveys are performed by an inspector
The potential exists for airborne contamination
Individuals routinely exceed 20 percent of the exposure limits
There were exposures greater than the 10 CFR Part 20 limits
Declaration of pregnancy must be voluntary and must be in writing.

constraints.text: References for this guidance are not to be used as requirements unless the licensee has committed to the specific guidance document in writing.
Sample sizes recommended are for broad planning purposes and are not rigid requirements.

The inspector is cautioned not to enter a high radiation area without a licensee escort trained in radiation protection who is knowledgeable of the high radiation area and the health physics precautions to be taken.
Licensee personnel dosimetry programs normally include provisions for monitoring beta, gamma, and fast and slow neutrons.
All instrumentation used during these activities are required to be operable, calibrated, and maintained as required by 10 CFR 20.1501(b) and the licensee's procedures.
Acceptable levels of contamination are normally specified in facility procedures, typically ranging from 100-1000 disintegrations per minute (dpm) per 100 square centimeters.
Records for survey, sampling and monitoring are required to be maintained in accordance with 10 CFR 20.2103.
The licensee is required to keep exposure records (e.g., TLD or film badge records) on form NRC-5 or equivalent (See 10 CFR 20.2106).
Changes in the facility or procedures described in the safety analysis report are required to be made in accordance with 10 CFR 50.59.
The declaration of pregnancy is revocable by the woman and the woman does not need to provide any 'medical proof' of pregnancy.
